pathophysiology_summary: A minor skin scratch has allowed bacteria (often polymicrobial or Group A Streptococcus) to invade the deep subcutaneous tissue and fascial planes, where they produce toxins causing rapid tissue destruction, thrombosis of local blood vessels, and gas production. Pain far out of proportion to visible skin findings occurs because the infection has already destroyed tissue and nerves beyond what is visible on the surface — this is the single most important early warning sign, often present before the more obvious findings of bullae or crepitus develop. Rapidly spreading erythema, haemorrhagic bullae, and skin necrosis reflect vascular thrombosis and tissue death, while fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and new confusion indicate progression to severe sepsis/septic shock. This is a fulminant, life- and limb-threatening surgical emergency: oral or even standard IV antibiotics alone cannot control the infection — urgent, often extensive, surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue is required alongside broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and aggressive resuscitation.

differential.evidence_basis: Pain out of proportion to a minor scratch with rapid spread.
Would be the default assumption for spreading redness after a scratch, but pain out of proportion strongly argues against simple cellulitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Pain out of proportion to visible findings should immediately raise necrotising fasciitis as leading, even before bullae/crepitus are known.

differential.diagnosis: Necrotising fasciitis with early septic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Haemorrhagic bullae (DI-003) and new confusion (DI-004) confirm advanced deep tissue necrosis with systemic sepsis.
Haemorrhagic bullae, rapid spread, and confusion are not features of simple cellulitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the case should be treated as an unequivocal surgical emergency requiring immediate transport.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring immediate emergency transport for surgical debridement and IV antibiotics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan offering only oral antibiotics is a critical, potentially fatal safety failure.

red_flags.description: Pain far out of proportion to a minor scratch, unresponsive to simple analgesia
red_flags.clinical_significance: The single most important early warning sign of necrotising fasciitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition allows further tissue destruction and progression to septic shock.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading erythema, visibly progressing within hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reflects the fulminant, rapidly progressive nature of the infection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Extensive tissue loss requiring more radical surgery, or death.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates advanced tissue necrosis and systemic infection.
Indicates evolving septic shock with cerebral hypoperfusion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to cardiovascular collapse and death.

red_flags.description: Subcutaneous crepitus, grey/purple skin discolouration, and paradoxical loss of sensation on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms gas-producing deep fascial infection with tissue necrosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Waiting for crepitus or bullae before escalating
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor should not wait for the full 'textbook' tetrad (crepitus, bullae) to escalate — pain out of proportion to visible findings alone, especially with rapid spread, should be sufficient to trigger emergency escalation, since necrotising fasciitis can progress from subtle to fulminant within hours.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct visualisation of the wound/skin discolouration
Sensation testing over the affected area
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 Priority 1 (Intensive Care Paramedic) dispatch for emergency surgical debridement and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; nil by mouth
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Assuming this is simple cellulitis and prescribing oral antibiotics risks catastrophic delay in life-saving surgical debridement.
